title: Motion
content: Motion is the change in position of an object with respect to time and a chosen reference point.

title: Reference Point
content: Motion is always described relative to a reference point or observer. An object may appear at rest to one observer and in motion to another.

title: Motion Depends on Time
content: If the position of an object changes with time relative to a reference point, the object is said to be in motion.

title: Examples
content: A moving car on a road, flying birds, flowing water in a river, rotating fan blades, and a swinging pendulum are all examples of motion.

title: Types of Motion
content: Translational Motion, Rectilinear Motion, Circular Motion, Rotational Motion, Periodic Motion, Oscillatory Motion, Random Motion

title: Rest and Motion
content: An object is said to be at rest if its position does not change with time relative to a chosen reference point.

title: Observer Dependence
content: The state of rest or motion of an object depends on the observer and the chosen frame of reference.

title: Path of Motion
content: The path followed by a moving object can be straight, circular, or irregular depending on the nature of motion.

title: Importance of Motion
content: Understanding motion is essential for studying speed, velocity, acceleration, force, and laws of motion.

title: Remember
content: Motion is relative, not absolute. Always mention the reference point while describing motion.

title: Translational Motion
content: Motion in which the entire object moves from one place to another and every point of the object covers the same distance in the same direction.

title: Whole Body Motion
content: In translational motion, all parts of the object move together.

title: Example
content: A car moving along a straight road shows translational motion.

title: Rectilinear Motion
content: Motion in which an object moves along a straight-line path.

title: Straight Path
content: The direction of motion does not change in rectilinear motion.

title: Example
content: A stone falling vertically downward shows rectilinear motion.

title: Circular Motion
content: Motion in which an object moves along a circular path around a fixed centre.

title: Fixed Radius
content: The distance of the object from the centre remains constant.

title: Example
content: The hands of a clock moving around the dial show circular motion.

title: Rotational Motion
content: Motion in which an object rotates about a fixed axis.

title: Axis of Rotation
content: Different points of the object move in circular paths around the axis.

title: Example
content: A ceiling fan rotating about its central rod shows rotational motion.

title: Periodic Motion
content: Motion that repeats itself at equal intervals of time.

title: Repeating Motion
content: The time after which motion repeats is called the time period.

title: Example
content: The motion of a swinging pendulum is periodic motion.

title: Oscillatory Motion
content: To and fro motion of an object about its mean position, repeated at regular intervals.

title: Mean Position
content: The object moves alternately on either side of the mean position.

title: Example
content: The motion of a simple pendulum is oscillatory motion.

title: Random Motion
content: Irregular motion in which an object does not follow a fixed path.

title: Irregular Path
content: The direction and speed of motion change randomly.

title: Example
content: The motion of gas molecules in air is random motion.

title: Distance
content: Distance is the total length of the actual path travelled by an object during motion.

title: Path Dependent
content: Distance depends on the actual path followed by the object.

title: Scalar Quantity
content: Distance is a scalar quantity and is always positive.

title: Example
content: If a person walks 5 m east and 5 m west, the total distance covered is 10 m.

title: Displacement
content: Displacement is the shortest straight-line distance between the initial and final positions of an object.

title: Shortest Distance
content: Displacement depends only on initial and final positions.

title: Vector Quantity
content: Displacement is a vector quantity as it has both magnitude and direction.

title: Example
content: If a person walks 5 m east and returns to the starting point, displacement is zero.
